text: Hi, this is Abu Bakr Ali. I'm presenting a case here of a gentleman with a splenic flexure mass. It was neuro-obstructing. They were able to pass a scope all the way to the cecum. He was discussed at the tumor board and reviewing his imaging, he had the tumor is very, very close to the stomach and looked like it was invading the stomach. So a decision was made to kind of start him a new adjuvant chemotherapy.

The surgeon opens the gastrocolic omentum to assess the tumor's proximity to the stomach. The tumor appears smaller post-chemotherapy but is adhered to the greater curvature of the stomach, prompting an en bloc resection. The surgeon uses a reinforced green load stapler to perform a wedge gastrectomy along the greater curve near the fundus. The final stapler is applied, ensuring good perfusion, though minor bleeding is observed at the staple line.

The surgeon mobilizes the descending colon using lateral to medial mobilization, retracts the greater omentum, and takes down the left branch of the middle colic artery. The colon appears dilated proximal to the tumor. The mesentery is taken down to create space, and the bowel is lined up in an isoperistaltic fashion for a side-to-side anastomosis. Sutures are used for retraction, and a stapler is placed along the suprapubic area. The surgeon performs the anastomosis, adjusting sutures for retraction and tying them to secure the common colotomy. Canal stitches are used to close the common colotomy, inverting the bowel.
The video shows the stomach and pancreas in the surgical field, with visible signs of perfusion in the area.
